title: Common definitions
description: Reusable building blocks shared by the content schemas (need-requirement, use-case, case-study, news) and the side vocabularies. Centralising these is what makes the content types one interlinked graph rather than disconnected collections. Note: tools/demonstrators are a distinct content kind but deliberately have no schema of their own - they validate against case-study.schema.json verbatim (see that schema's description), distinguished only by which content/ folder they live in.

identifier.description: Unique, stable, URL-safe identifier for an item. Stable across edits; use 'supersedes' relations rather than re-using ids.

summary.description: Short, plain-text gist for listing cards, search results, graph tooltips, and social sharing. Must not contain Markdown. (Aligned to 280 chars across all three content types.)

markdownText.description: Long-form body text. Supports Markdown (headings, lists, links, emphasis, images, inline code).

language.description: BCP-47 / ISO language code of the human-readable content, e.g. 'en', 'lv', 'cs'.

editorialStatus.description: Publication lifecycle of a website content object (use cases, case studies). Tracks editorial workflow, NOT engineering acceptance.

itemStatus.description: Engineering lifecycle of a need/requirement item. Distinct from editorialStatus on purpose.

newsType.description: Category of a news item, shown as a label on listing cards. Open-ended on purpose (includes 'other') since news topics are more varied than the controlled vocabularies elsewhere; add new values here as recurring categories emerge rather than letting news.schema.json drift out of alignment with the rest.

maturity.description: Deployment maturity of the application pattern (use case) or the specific deployment described (case study). concept: idea/feasibility only. pilot: limited trial. demonstrated: validated in a real setting. operational: in routine use. scaled: rolled out across many sites/users.

priority.description: Relative importance (MoSCoW). Use a project-specific numeric scale in attributes if finer granularity is needed.

confidence.description: How confident the author/stakeholders are that the item is correctly captured or specified.

targetKind.description: Which collection a relation target lives in. 'need' and 'requirement' share one schema but are distinguished here because the graph treats them differently. 'tool' validates against case-study.schema.json (a tool is presented the same editorial way as a case study) but is kept as a distinct pool/kind because it is something a visitor opens and uses, not a story about a past deployment; which folder a file lives in (content/tools/ vs content/casestudies/) is what actually distinguishes the two, not the field shape. Links to side-vocabulary entries (products, market segments) are expressed via 'classification', not via relations.

relationType.description:
Unified, directional relation vocabulary spanning the whole graph. Direction is source -> target.

DEMAND-SIDE (typically need/requirement -> need/requirement):
- derives_from: source is justified by a higher-level need (no part-whole structure).
- conflicts_with: source is logically/practically incompatible with target (symmetric in meaning, declared once).
- duplicates: symmetric equivalence with target.
- consolidates: source synthesises/merges target (many raw items -> one; mark target itemStatus 'superseded').
- part_of: source is one child of an AND-decomposition of target (one parent -> many children).

GRAPH-WIDE:
- depends_on: source cannot be satisfied/realised unless target is too (any kind -> any kind).
- supersedes: source is a newer version of a same-kind target (mark target status superseded/archived).
- related: generic, symmetric thematic association (any kind -> any kind); declared one-directionally.

SATISFACTION / EVIDENCE (the cross-type connective tissue):
- satisfied_by: a need/requirement is fulfilled by target (e.g. a use_case or external artifact).
- addresses: a use_case provides a way to meet target (use_case -> need/requirement). Conceptual inverse of satisfied_by; prefer declaring from the use_case side.
- instantiates: a case_study is a concrete real-world instance of target (case_study -> use_case).
- illustrates: a case_study is a real-world example of target (case_study -> need).
- demonstrates: a case_study shows target being met in practice (case_study -> requirement).

target.description: The id of the related item.

note.description: Optional short annotation for this edge (e.g. shown as a graph tooltip). Plain text.

relationList.description: Typed links to other items in the graph. Every relation declares target_kind so the front end can resolve and render edges uniformly.

classification.description: Subject facets used for faceted search, filtering, and recommendation across ALL content types. Controlled facets reference side-vocabulary ids; the rest are open tag lists. Use 'tags' (top-level) for free keywords that are not facets.

products.description: Product taxonomy ids from vocab/products.json, referenced uniformly at ANY level: a service ('clms'), a mission ('sentinel-1'), a product ('egms'), or a sub-product ('egms-ortho'). The EO data/services the item is about or relies on. Filter UIs should expand by taxonomy ancestry so a query on 'clms' also matches items tagged 'egms' or 'egms-ortho'.

regions.description: Geographic scope, e.g. 'Czechia', 'European Union', 'global'. Free text for now; promote to a vocabulary later if needed.

organisation_types.description: Types of organisation involved, e.g. 'public authority', 'SME', 'infrastructure operator', 'regulator'.

outcome_types.description: Kind of result, e.g. 'cost reduction', 'risk mitigation', 'regulatory compliance', 'situational awareness'.

site.description: Escape hatch for fields a single website needs that aren't shared cross-content facets and don't belong in the common schema (e.g. a Copernicus integration-mode pill, an 'is this a real EU project' flag, a project link). Keep this small; if a key here turns out to be useful across content types or sites, promote it to a real classification facet instead. Unlike the other classification properties, values are not constrained to string arrays.

stakeholder.description: A real-world person, role, or organisation associated with an item (NOT an abstract persona; use 'actors' on a use case for those).

actor.description: An abstract role/persona that participates in a use case (e.g. 'bridge asset manager'), as opposed to a named real-world stakeholder.

description.description: What this actor does in the use case and what they get out of it.

outcome.description: A quotable result statement, with an optional quantified figure. Used for expected benefits (use case) and demonstrated key results (case study).

metric.description: Optional quantified figure, e.g. '40% faster inspection cycle'.

type.description: Unified provenance vocabulary across all content types.

reference.description: Citation, URL, document id, or person.

history.description: Append-only event log. The first entry is creation; every later entry is an update. Derive created_at/updated_at from history[0] and history[-1] rather than storing separate fields.

comment.description: What changed and why. Supports Markdown.
